text:
Week 2 - Scripts, Functions & Refactoring


Show code
library(palmerpenguins)
library(dplyr)



Attache Paket: 'dplyr'


Die folgenden Objekte sind maskiert von 'package:stats':

    filter, lag


Die folgenden Objekte sind maskiert von 'package:base':

    intersect, setdiff, setequal, union


Show code
mean_mass_by <- function(data, group_var) {
  data |>
    group_by({{group_var}}) |>
    summarise(mean_mass = mean(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE))
}

mean_mass_by(penguins, species)


# A tibble: 3 × 2
  species   mean_mass
  <fct>         <dbl>
1 Adelie        3701.
2 Chinstrap     3733.
3 Gentoo        5076.


Expected to do: calculate the mean mass of penguins grouped by species
What might go wrong: group_var is not treated as an variable that will be inserted but as an actual “name”.


Show code
summarise_species <- function(data) {
  data |>
    group_by(species) |>
    summarise(mean_mass = mean_body_mass(body_mass_g))
}
 
mean_body_mass <- function(x) {
  mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)   
}
 
summarise_species(penguins)


# A tibble: 3 × 2
  species   mean_mass
  <fct>         <dbl>
1 Adelie        3701.
2 Chinstrap     3733.
3 Gentoo        5076.




Show code
my_factorial <- function(n) {
  stopifnot( n >= 0, n == as.integer(n))
  if (n <= 1) return(1)
  return(n * my_factorial(n - 1))
}

my_factorial(5)   # returns 120 — correct


[1] 120


Show code

[1] 1

Show code
standardise <- function(x) {
  (x - mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)) / sd(x, na.rm = TRUE)
}

standardise(c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5))   # fine


[1] -1.2649111 -0.6324555  0.0000000  0.6324555  1.2649111


Show code
standardise(c(1, 2, NA, 4, 5))  # returns all NAs — why?


[1] -1.0954451 -0.5477226         NA  0.5477226  1.0954451


debugonce() enters debugging once after the function is called and then never again.
debug(standardise) enters debugging everytime the function standardise is called. To not enter debugging after calling standardise you have to call undebug(standardise)
Scenario where debug() is prefered over debugonce():
When fixing a bug and planning on calling the function many times over.
You are debugging standardise() inside a larger analysis pipeline, and each small code change requires rerunning the function to see whether the problem is fixed.

title: Reflection Log
text:
Week 3 - Debugging

Did you use an LLM to help debug? What did you share with it, and what approach did it take? Was the answer correct? How did you verify it?

I had to fix an error and try the debugging methods. The error was shown on RStudio but I couldn’t see what was wrong. I pasted the code and error message into the LLM and asked it to explain the message and find which line is faulty. After I could fix the error myself.
For testing the debug method I also had the LLM explain the methods first, since I those were new to me. I tried to use the methods myself but couldn’t really figure it out. I asked the LLM on how to actually use them.

title: Reflection Log
text:
Week 4 - Version Control & Collaborative

Describe a confusing or frustrating moment with git this week. How did you resolve it? What do you now understand that you didn’t before?

A frustrating moment was when initialising git. The main branch was called mian which was a setting mispell. Additionally the README section was filled with ‘statprog’ 4 times which wasn’t supposed to be there. I tried fixing it with the help of an LLM but it did more trouble than good. So I decided to delete the original repository and start over. The same mistake of the missspelled main branch happened again. I decided to look into the settings and found that the name of the original branch, when creating the repository was set to ‘mian’. I was able to set it to ‘main’ and create the original branch with the right name. I moved on to connecting the repository to my folder. It took me a few tries and hours to fix the mistakes, but I was able to get the repository clean.

How does committing code frequently change how you think about your work? Have you noticed yourself structuring tasks differently because of it?

It makes me think more structured. Completing / Commiting one task first and then moving on to the next one to ensure that the structure doesn’t get lost and all task get done cleanly.

title: Reflection Log
text:
Week 5 - Quarto Websites & Collaborative Coding

What is the difference between a Quarto document and a Quarto website project? Describe in your own words what _quarto.yml does and how it changes how you think about organising a multi-file project.

A quarto document is a single page, where as a quarto website is a collection of different quarto documents.
_quarto.yml is the control settings of the whole Quarto website and structures everything. Instead of having to write the settings of the page at the top everytime, it is defined once in the _quarto.yml file. In my opinion, it is much more efficient and gives a structured look since the settings are the same across the website.

Walk through one format: or execute: option you set in _quarto.yml this week. Why did you choose that option, and what effect did it have on the rendered output?

The format is html because I want the output to be a website.
code-fold: true - the code will not be directly shown. The user can click on the expandable to show the code. I chose this because it gives a more clean look instead of having pages of code.
code-summary: “Show code” - This message will appear on the expandable to specify that there is code and an option to show it.
code-line-numbers: true - It shows the line numbers inside of the code chunks. Also for structure.
code-copy: hover - When the user hovers over a code chunk it allows them to copy the code.
theme: - aesthetics of the website

cosmo - theme name
brand - allows custom changes on top of the cosmo theme

css: styles.css - a file that includes specific, custom style changes that are applied to every page
toc: true - Table of contents on the right side

Describe a situation this week where you used a git branch, stash, or pull request. What problem were you solving, and would you approach it the same way again?

I was editing the main proposal file and was working on the main branch. Luckily only I was editing and pushing the changes so there were no conflicts but it could happen that the other teammate works on the branch and then it’ll lead to a merge conflict. To avoid this we decided to use individual branches when working on the project since it will lead to a cleaner history.
++ In week 8 I merged a feature branch into main and deleted the remote branch on GitHub. I continued working on that same branch locally and forgot that the remote tracking was gone. When I tried to push an error message appeared. I was very confused because I didn’t realize the remote branch no longer exists. In my attempt to fix the ‘sync’ issue, I improperly performed a pull that overwrote my local progress. I learned the importance of structured branches and frequent committing and pushing instead of doing the whole project on just one branch and pushing everything at once.
Next time we could resolve that problem like this:

git branch shows I am on main instead of feature branch
git log —oneline --graph --all -n 10 see if commits exist locally
git fetch origin & git log --oneline origin/branchname..branchname check what was pushed –> If list of commits returns it means those commits exist locally and have not been pushed to the remote branch yet
git push -u origin branchname –> push changes from the branch I’m actually on

title: Reflection Log
text:
Week 6 - R Packages

Can you think of packages you have used that are very large or very small? Why do you think they contain many or few functions?
How do the ideas of outside-in and inside-out function design relate to package design?
devtools::load_all() reloads your package without restarting R, whereas source()-ing a file just runs it in the global environment. Did you notice a difference in behaviour when using load_all()? What does it tell you about how packages work differently from plain scripts?
Writing roxygen2 documentation (@param, @returns, @examples) forces you to describe your function from a user’s perspective before you’ve finished writing it. Did documenting a function change how you thought about its interface or arguments?

title: Reflection Log
section: Week 7 - Initial Data Analysis & Data Cleaning
text:
Week 7 - Initial Data Analysis & Data Cleaning

What data format or file type did you encounter that was unfamiliar? How did you read it into R, and what did you learn in the process?
It was unfamiliar that we could just load the data from a different folder inside the project (/data/raw/…). It’s quite logical.
Did you ask an LLM about data licensing, provenance, or quality? What question did you ask, and how did you assess whether the answer was trustworthy?
We didn’t have to ask an LLM since the practical had an example of a licence of a dataset from the same source (Stadt München). We looked into the description and licence section of our Dataset and noticed the two were very similar. So we compared them and it happened to be identical to the licence from the practical. If we do ask LLMs question about our project we make sure to skim the source / information the LLM is refering to. Most of the time we use LLMs to save time so we don’t have to read pages of documentation. The LLM primarely helps find the location of the information.
Describe one visualisation you made during IDA. What did you learn from it that a summary statistic alone wouldn’t have revealed?
We made a visualisation of the unemployment trend over time. It showed us how the rate dropped or increased over the years. While a summary statistic includes the same numbers and one could draw the same conclusions the visualisation shows where the highs and lows were and the overall trend at one glance.
What question did your data raise that you hadn’t anticipated at the start? Do you think it’s worth pursuing, and why?
The data showed an abnormal high rate in the year 2005. The dataset alone couldn’t explain it and so we decided to research. It turned out to be this high because of a reform that counted the unemployed and people on welfare into one number starting in 2005. After this new rules were introduced where receiving welfare became stricter and so the numbers decreased again. We decided to not pursue this question or include it into one of our RQs since it can’t be explained by our data alone and we would have to use sources from outside.

title: Reflection Log
text:
Week 9 - Reproducibility, Open Data & renv

Have you set up renv in a project (course or personal)? Describe one dependency or reproducibility issue it helped you avoid or document — or one challenge you encountered when getting it to work.
Yes, we set up renv in our project. It helped us document our package versions in renv.lock, so the project can run the same way for everyone. I mostly used snapshot() after installing new packages. I didn’t use restore() consistently yet, so I sometimes installed missing packages manually instead. That’s something I want to fix going forward.
Did you use a dataset with an explicit open licence (e.g. CC-BY, ODbL, PDDL)? What did the licence allow or restrict, and did that affect how you plan to share or cite it in your final project?
Yes, we used a dataset under the Datenlizenz Deutschland Namensnennung 2.0 licence. It allows us to copy, use and redistribute the data, even commercially, as long as we attribute the source. This means we just need to cite Statistisches Amt München properly in our final project. This way it doesn’t affect our final project.
Describe one documentation gap you discovered in your own or a team member’s analysis — something a future reader could not have reproduced or verified without asking you. How did you (or would you) fill it?
One gap was in the column explenation. The raw file has columns like Basiswert 1, Basiswert 2 and Auspragung with no clear explanation of what they mean. A future reader couldn’t reproduce our filtering choices without the external metadata PDF, since it explains that Basiswert 2 changes meaning depending on Auspragung. We filled this gap by documenting it directly in our proposal, so readers don’t have to track down the PDF themselves.

title: Reflection Log
text:
Week 10 - Analysis & Inference

Describe an analysis decision you made — model choice, variable inclusion, aggregation level, etc. What alternatives did you consider, and why did you choose what you did?
For RQ1, we decided to group the 25 districts into five city areas instead of plotting all districts individually. We considered keeping all 25 districts separate, but that would need 25 plots and be hard to interpret. Grouping into five areas let us still see regional differences without overfitting to noise at the individual district level.
For RQ2, we changed our initial plan from just looking at South Munich to looking all 25 districts. We made this decision because when we ran the Hypotheses test, the result were strongly against the Null Hypothesis that there is no systematic disparity between women and men in unemployment. We became a bit skeptic because the sample size was also very small and so we ran the test on Munich as a whole. The result were very different with no evidence that there is this systematic disparity. We decided on keeping the second one since it was more reliable because of the sample size (25 instead of 6 districts). Additionally, we used a simulation based permutation test instead of a t-test, because rates / probabilities are often skewed and don’t follow a normal- or t-distribution.
If you used an LLM to help reason through a statistical question, describe the exchange. Did the LLM’s reasoning hold up? Where did it help, and where did you need to apply your own judgement?
We used Gemini to help understand the infer function syntax. The first explanation was too complex, so we asked for a simpler version. That helped us understand what each function does, but we still had to apply our own judgement when debugging, since we had errors from using the wrong variable names. The LLM helped explain the syntax, not the actual statistical reasoning behind our result.
For the {infer} test in RQ2, I used Google Gemini to discuss whether a theory-based t-distribution was appropriate given we have percentages. Based on this discussion, I reviewed the assumptions of the t-test versus a simulation based permutation approach on https://moderndive.com/v2/hypothesis-testing.html and decided to implement the simulation based test.

title: Reflection Log
text:
Week 11 — Statistical Communication & Visualisation

What visualisation principle or technique did you apply this week that you hadn’t used before? Where did you learn it (lecture, reading, LLM, experimentation)?
We applied the table versus plot decision this week. Before, we would default to plots for everything. Now we used a gt table specifically for the RQ2 comparison, because a table showed the exact means more clearly than a plot would. We learned this in the W11 lecture and practiced it first on the palmerpenguins sandbox in the practical.
Describe a figure you revised or improved. What changed between the first and final version, and what drove each change?
We revised our RQ plots by adding captions and interpretations to them. The first versions just showed the plot with a title and no further context. In the lecture and practical, we were taught to add captions and descriptions so a reader can understand the finding without having to interpret the plot themselves. This drove us to add captions and short interpretation sentences to our RQ1 and RQ2 plots, so each figure states what it actually shows instead of leaving that up to the reader.
Think of a table or plot you made (or encountered) this week. Would the other format have communicated the same information better or worse — and why? If you used {gt}, what was one formatting choice you made deliberately (column labels, decimal places, spanners, source notes) and what guided it?
For RQ2, a gt table communicated the difference in means better than a plot would, since the exact mean values are displayed. If we had used a plot instead, it would be harder to read off precise numbers. One deliberate gt choice was setting decimals to 1 for the rate columns, since more decimals would be false precision for a percentage like this.

title: Group Reflection
text:
For each skill area, describe what your group actually used it for. If you did not use a skill, say so and note why.


What we did:
We practices writing functions in Practical3.qmd. We wrote a grouped summary function using tidy evaluation (mean_mass_by() with ({{ }})-> searches for column name not the variable literally named) and a recursive function. We did not use custom functions into the group project itself. The actual analysis in proposal.qmd and report.qmd stayed as inline dplyr pipe chain code.
What worked well:
The practical exercise gave us a understanding of tidy evaluation ({{ }}) and recursion, which we hadn’t used before. Because it was separate from the project’s real analysis, we could focus on understanding without worrying about breaking anything that mattered.
What was hard:
Looking back, proposal.qmd has two places with duplicated code. The missingness check (count(Auspragung) and the NA summary) is repeated almost exactly. We run it once on the full dataset and again on the South Munich subset. The district code extraction (str_extract(Raumbezug, "^[0-9]+")) is also repeated. It appears in the RQ1 area mapping and again in the RQ2 South filter.
We didn’t notice this while writing the code. By the time we did, the deadline was close. Refactoring working analysis code just to make it tidier felt risky. We decided not to risk introducing a new bug just for style.



What we did:
An example of a bug we encountered in the project is that rendered plot images were shown as broken/missing on the live GitHub Pages site. Our process through bugs was the same each time. We first read the actual error / looked at the problem and then checked the lecture/practical material for the topic involved (Quarto deployment for images) and attempted fixes ourselves. Only when that didn’t resolve it or made things worse, we asked an LLM how we could fix the problem and the cause.
For the broken images bug, trying to fix it ourselves didn’t get us far. The cause was a *_files/ rule in .gitignore silently excluding the rendered figure folders from every commit, so the HTML pages referenced image files that had never actually been pushed to the repo. The LLM correctly recognized this from the description alone, which we then confirmed by checking our actual .gitignore contents.
What worked well:
Trying to solve the problems ourselves first, using the actual error / problem and course materials, meant we had rough ideas what the fix / root could be for each bug before asking for help. This way we didn’t blindly trust the LLM and instead could make sense out of the explanation.
What was hard:
Most of the times the root causes weren’t obvious from what showed up alone. We wouldn’t have found the actual culprit by just looking at the code longer. The .gitignore was something we had set up at the very start of the project and forgot about since we didn’t fully understand what it does until actually deploying a website.



What we did:
We used Git for version control throughout the project, working with branches, commits, and a remote GitHub repository. Early on we hit a repo setup problem: the default branch was misspelled as “mian” instead of “main,” and the README had a stray placeholder word repeated several times. We tried fixing it with an LLM’s help, but the fix caused more problems than it solved, so we deleted the repository and started over. The same “mian” typo happened again on the second attempt, which led us to check GitHub’s repository creation settings directly rather than trying to fix it after the fact. We found the default branch name setting and corrected it there.
What worked well:
Once we adopted separate branches per task instead of everyone working directly on main, the workflow got noticeably cleaner and we avoided merge conflicts. Committing frequently also made it easier to structure work by finishing and committing one task before moving to the next kept the project from feeling chaotic.
What was hard:
Later in the project, we also ran into a remote tracking issue. After merging a feature branch into main and deleting the remote branch on GitHub, one of us kept working on that same branch locally without noticing the remote tracking was gone. A later push failed, and trying to resolve it, an improper pull overwrote local progress. We learned to check git branch and git status before continuing working.



What we did:
Everyone in the group worked on _quarto.yml. We didn’t split it up by person. Our workflow was to render the site locally first and check it looked right. Then we pushed it. GitHub Pages only updates from the rendered output, not the source files. So if we forgot to render before pushing, the live site would be out of date.
What worked well:
The theme (cosmo + brand) and the sidebar with the TOC worked well right away. No real problems there.
What was hard:
It took us a while to get used to the render-then-push workflow. We didn’t fully understand at first that GitHub Pages needs the rendered HTML, not just the source files pushed. This caused some early confusion.



What we did:
We agreed on the basic packages as a group early on (tidyverse, readr). As the project went on, everyone added more packages individually when they needed them, like visdat, skimr and naniar for data screening, infer for the hypothesis test, and gt for the summary table.
What worked well:
Most packages were straightforward to use since we already knew them from the practicals or the documentation was clear enough (skimr, naniar, gt).
What was hard:
infer was hard to understand at first. The syntax with specify(), hypothesize() and shade_p_value() was not obvious. We followed the practicals template as a sandbox first and tried to understand the functions by testing them there before applying them to our own data in the project.



What we did:
We used vis_dat, skim, miss_var_summary and vis_miss to screen the dataset. We found no unexpected column types, categorical variables were <chr> and numbers were <dbl>. We identified two data quality issues. First, individual districts and the Stadt München total were mixed in the same Raumbezug column. Second, the Auspragung column combined gender and totals (insgesamt) in one long column. We also found an external metadata PDF linked under the CSV file on the main data page, which explained that Basiswert 2 changes meaning depending on Auspragung, and that Stadt München rows aggregate differently than individual district rows.
What worked well:
The screening tools (skim, vis_dat, naniar) gave us a fast overview and made it easy to spot the missingness and structure issues early.
What was hard:
Getting familiar with the column names and what they actually meant took time, since names like Basiswert 1, Basiswert 2 and Auspragung gave no information on their own. It wasn’t hard once we got familiar and worked with the data some time.



What we did:
We initialized renv in the project and committed renv.lock and the renv/ folder to the repo. When installing new packages, renv asked for a yes or no confirmation, and we followed that workflow with snapshot() to update the lockfile. For the dataset itself, we used data under the Datenlizenz Deutschland Namensnennung 2.0 license, which allows use and redistribution as long as the source is attributed. The license is public, so we didn’t run into any issues.
What worked well:
Committing the lockfile itself was simple and snapshot() picked up our packages without issues.
What was hard:
The renv syntax was confusing at first, especially the yes or no prompts when installing new packages. We also didn’t consistently use renv::restore() after pulling changes from each other, so we didn’t fully close the loop on reproducibility. We mostly relied on installing packages manually if something was missing, instead of restoring from the lockfile. We intend on using restore() from now on.



What we did:
For RQ1, we mapped each district to one of five city areas (Center, North, East, South, West) using the district code, joined that onto the dataset, and calculated annual mean unemployment rates per area from 2000 to 2024. We added Stadt München back in separately as a “Total” line. We produced a faceted line plot, plus an interactive plotly version with hover tooltips showing the exact year and rate.
For RQ2, we ran a difference in means test comparing männlich and weiblich unemployment rates in Munich for 2024, using infer with a simulation based permutation null distribution (5000 reps), shade_p_value(), and a significance level of \(\alpha = 0.05\). We also built a summary gt table.
We originally considered a third question, whether we could build a simple model to predict future unemployment percentages. We cut this to keep the project focused on two questions, in line with the course guidance to limit the proposal to at most two RQs.
What worked well:
The area mapping and annual means calculation for RQ1 worked cleanly. RQ2 results gave us a concrete p-value for the Hypothesis decision and a plot for visualization.
What was hard:
For RQ2, we changed our initial plan from just looking at South Munich to looking at all 25 districts. We made this decision because when we ran the Hypotheses test, the result was strongly against the Null Hypothesis, that there is no systematic disparity between women and men in unemployment. We became a bit skeptic because the sample size was also very small and so we ran the test on Munich as a whole. The result was very different with no evidence that there is this systematic disparity. We decided on keeping the second one since it was more reliable because of the sample size (25 instead of 6 districts). Additionally, we used a simulation based permutation test instead of a t-test, because rates / probabilities are often skewed and don’t follow a normal- or t-distribution.
We used Gemini to help understand the infer function syntax because it was hard to understand just from the practical. The first explanation was too complex, so we asked for a simpler version. That helped us understand what each function does, but we still had to apply our own judgement when debugging, since we had errors from using the wrong variable names and our functions were not complete. The LLM helped explain the syntax, not the actual statistical reasoning behind our result.
For the {infer} test, we used Google Gemini to discuss whether a theory-based t-distribution was appropriate given we have percentages. Based on this discussion, we rethought using the t-test versus a simulation based permutation approach on https://moderndive.com/v2/hypothesis-testing.html and decided to implement the simulation based test.



What we did:
In the sandbox part with palmerpenguins, we practiced deciding when to use a table versus a plot, building a gt summary table, and polishing a default ggplot2 plot. For our own project, we built a gt summary table for the RQ2 gender comparison, improved the captions on our plots, and added #| warning: false to clean up the chunk output. We also fixed the duplicate plot issue in our final report.qmd, keeping the interactive version and removing the older static version. We shared the gt table for RQ2 with the group, since it made the difference in means between männlich and weiblich easy to see at a glance instead of just reading it out of the infer output.
What worked well:
The gt table made the RQ2 result much clearer than the raw infer output alone. Once we knew the workflow from the sandbox exercise, applying it to our own table went smoothly.
What was hard:
Nothing major here, most of the work was applying what we practiced in the sandbox directly to our own project.

The LLM correctly recognized this from the description alone, which we then confirmed by checking our actual .gitignore contents.
What worked well:
Trying to solve the problems ourselves first, using the actual error / problem and course materials, meant we had rough ideas what the fix / root could be for each bug before asking for help. This way we didn’t blindly trust the LLM and instead could make sense out of the explanation.
What was hard:
Most of the times the root causes weren’t obvious from what showed up alone. We wouldn’t have found the actual culprit by just looking at the code longer. The .gitignore was something we had set up at the very start of the project and forgot about since we didn’t fully understand what it does until actually deploying a website.



What we did:
We used Git for version control throughout the project, working with branches, commits, and a remote GitHub repository. Early on we hit a repo setup problem: the default branch was misspelled as “mian” instead of “main,” and the README had a stray placeholder word repeated several times. We tried fixing it with an LLM’s help, but the fix caused more problems than it solved, so we deleted the repository and started over. The same “mian” typo happened again on the second attempt, which led us to check GitHub’s repository creation settings directly rather than trying to fix it after the fact. We found the default branch name setting and corrected it there.
What worked well:
Once we adopted separate branches per task instead of everyone working directly on main, the workflow got noticeably cleaner and we avoided merge conflicts. Committing frequently also made it easier to structure work by finishing and committing one task before moving to the next kept the project from feeling chaotic.
What was hard:
Later in the project, we also ran into a remote tracking issue. After merging a feature branch into main and deleting the remote branch on GitHub, one of us kept working on that same branch locally without noticing the remote tracking was gone. A later push failed, and trying to resolve it, an improper pull overwrote local progress. We learned to check git branch and git status before continuing working.



What we did:
Everyone in the group worked on _quarto.yml. We didn’t split it up by person. Our workflow was to render the site locally first and check it looked right. Then we pushed it. GitHub Pages only updates from the rendered output, not the source files. So if we forgot to render before pushing, the live site would be out of date.
What worked well:
The theme (cosmo + brand) and the sidebar with the TOC worked well right away. No real problems there.
What was hard:
It took us a while to get used to the render-then-push workflow. We didn’t fully understand at first that GitHub Pages needs the rendered HTML, not just the source files pushed. This caused some early confusion.



What we did:
We agreed on the basic packages as a group early on (tidyverse, readr). As the project went on, everyone added more packages individually when they needed them, like visdat, skimr and naniar for data screening, infer for the hypothesis test, and gt for the summary table.
What worked well:
Most packages were straightforward to use since we already knew them from the practicals or the documentation was clear enough (skimr, naniar, gt).
What was hard:
infer was hard to understand at first. The syntax with specify(), hypothesize() and shade_p_value() was not obvious. We followed the practicals template as a sandbox first and tried to understand the functions by testing them there before applying them to our own data in the project.



What we did:
We used vis_dat, skim, miss_var_summary and vis_miss to screen the dataset. We found no unexpected column types, categorical variables were <chr> and numbers were <dbl>. We identified two data quality issues. First, individual districts and the Stadt München total were mixed in the same Raumbezug column. Second, the Auspragung column combined gender and totals (insgesamt) in one long column. We also found an external metadata PDF linked under the CSV file on the main data page, which explained that Basiswert 2 changes meaning depending on Auspragung, and that Stadt München rows aggregate differently than individual district rows.
What worked well:
The screening tools (skim, vis_dat, naniar) gave us a fast overview and made it easy to spot the missingness and structure issues early.
What was hard:
Getting familiar with the column names and what they actually meant took time, since names like Basiswert 1, Basiswert 2 and Auspragung gave no information on their own. It wasn’t hard once we got familiar and worked with the data some time.



What we did:
We initialized renv in the project and committed renv.lock and the renv/ folder to the repo. When installing new packages, renv asked for a yes or no confirmation, and we followed that workflow with snapshot() to update the lockfile. For the dataset itself, we used data under the Datenlizenz Deutschland Namensnennung 2.0 license, which allows use and redistribution as long as the source is attributed. The license is public, so we didn’t run into any issues.
What worked well:
Committing the lockfile itself was simple and snapshot() picked up our packages without issues.
What was hard:
The renv syntax was confusing at first, especially the yes or no prompts when installing new packages. We also didn’t consistently use renv::restore() after pulling changes from each other, so we didn’t fully close the loop on reproducibility. We mostly relied on installing packages manually if something was missing, instead of restoring from the lockfile. We intend on using restore() from now on.



What we did:
For RQ1, we mapped each district to one of five city areas (Center, North, East, South, West) using the district code, joined that onto the dataset, and calculated annual mean unemployment rates per area from 2000 to 2024. We added Stadt München back in separately as a “Total” line. We produced a faceted line plot, plus an interactive plotly version with hover tooltips showing the exact year and rate.
For RQ2, we ran a difference in means test comparing männlich and weiblich unemployment rates in Munich for 2024, using infer with a simulation based permutation null distribution (5000 reps), shade_p_value(), and a significance level of \(\alpha = 0.05\). We also built a summary gt table.
We originally considered a third question, whether we could build a simple model to predict future unemployment percentages. We cut this to keep the project focused on two questions, in line with the course guidance to limit the proposal to at most two RQs.
What worked well:
The area mapping and annual means calculation for RQ1 worked cleanly. RQ2 results gave us a concrete p-value for the Hypothesis decision and a plot for visualization.
What was hard:
For RQ2, we changed our initial plan from just looking at South Munich to looking at all 25 districts. We made this decision because when we ran the Hypotheses test, the result was strongly against the Null Hypothesis, that there is no systematic disparity between women and men in unemployment. We became a bit skeptic because the sample size was also very small and so we ran the test on Munich as a whole. The result was very different with no evidence that there is this systematic disparity. We decided on keeping the second one since it was more reliable because of the sample size (25 instead of 6 districts). Additionally, we used a simulation based permutation test instead of a t-test, because rates / probabilities are often skewed and don’t follow a normal- or t-distribution.
We used Gemini to help understand the infer function syntax because it was hard to understand just from the practical. The first explanation was too complex, so we asked for a simpler version. That helped us understand what each function does, but we still had to apply our own judgement when debugging, since we had errors from using the wrong variable names and our functions were not complete. The LLM helped explain the syntax, not the actual statistical reasoning behind our result.
For the {infer} test, we used Google Gemini to discuss whether a theory-based t-distribution was appropriate given we have percentages. Based on this discussion, we rethought using the t-test versus a simulation based permutation approach on https://moderndive.com/v2/hypothesis-testing.html and decided to implement the simulation based test.



What we did:
In the sandbox part with palmerpenguins, we practiced deciding when to use a table versus a plot, building a gt summary table, and polishing a default ggplot2 plot. For our own project, we built a gt summary table for the RQ2 gender comparison, improved the captions on our plots, and added #| warning: false to clean up the chunk output. We also fixed the duplicate plot issue in our final report.qmd, keeping the interactive version and removing the older static version. We shared the gt table for RQ2 with the group, since it made the difference in means between männlich and weiblich easy to see at a glance instead of just reading it out of the infer output.
What worked well:
The gt table made the RQ2 result much clearer than the raw infer output alone. Once we knew the workflow from the sandbox exercise, applying it to our own table went smoothly.
What was hard:
Nothing major here, most of the work was applying what we practiced in the sandbox directly to our own project.

title: Group Reflection
text:
Key analytical decisions

Decision 1: Expanding RQ2 to all 25 Munich districts
What were we deciding between?
We were deciding whether to test the gender gap in unemployment across all of Munich, or narrow it to one specific area (South) and what {infer} test to use.
How did we reason through it?
We decided on Munich as a whole, since focusing on just South Munich’s districts would limit the sample size to just 6 districts. This could bear the risk of including or running into distorted noise data. To have a more stable test and output we decided on the biggest possible sample.
For the test we discussed with Google Gemini and verifyed the recommendation of a simulation based permutation test by looking through ModernDive’s hypothesis testing chapter .
In hindsight, would we decide differently?
No, we’re happy with the scope, since it gave us a clear, focused result instead of a by chance distorted mislead and the test delivered results without assumptions of a distribution.


Decision 2: Grouping districts into five city areas for RQ1
What were we deciding between?
We were deciding between plotting all 25 districts individually or grouping them into fewer, larger regions.
How did we reason through it?
25 individual plots would be hard to interpret at a glance. We built our own mapping of districts into five city areas (Center, North, East, South, West) so we could still see regional differences without having to look at 25 individual plots. We also kept the Stadt München total as its own separate “Total” category for completion.
In hindsight, would we decide differently?
No, this grouping is a good balance of not too much detail and not too little.


Decision 3: Cutting RQ3
What were we deciding between?
We were deciding between keeping a third research question on predicting future unemployment percentages or limiting the project to just two RQs.
How did we reason through it?
We decided to cut it to keep the project focused on two questions, in line with the course guidance to limit the proposal to at most two RQs. We also noticed that a prediction would be risky, because events outside the data, like economic shocks, could have a major impact on the trend and can’t be captured by a simple model.
In hindsight, would we decide differently?
No, cutting it was the right call, since it let us go deeper on RQ1 and RQ2 instead of spreading effort across three questions.

title: Group Reflection
text:
Workflow & collaboration
How did we divide work across group members?
We split tasks within each research question rather than assigning whole RQs to one person. For example, for RQ2, Rebecca built the gt table while Anna programmed the infer test. This let us work in parallel on the same question instead of one person owning it end to end.
What branching or Git workflow did we actually use — and did it match the proposal?
We mostly followed the plan from the proposal, using separate branches per task (e.g. feature/RQ1) and having another teammate verify before merging. This didn’t always hold up though. When something needed to be fixed fast or done quickly, we pushed directly to main without going through review. So the branching workflow was consistent for planned work but broke down under time pressure.
What would we change about how we coordinated?
We would structure task assignments more clearly. Right now we mostly divided work informally, saying things like “I’ll do this, you do that, could you look into that,” instead of writing tasks down with clear ownership and scope. More structure here would reduce the chance of things falling through the cracks or people working on overlapping parts by accident.

title: Group Reflection
text:
LLM usage
Describe up to 5 significant uses of an LLM tool during the project.

Usage 1: Debugging GitHub Pages not updating

Tool / model: Google Gemini
What it produced: A list of possible reasons why the site wasn’t updating. After we gave more context (that everything was already pushed to the repository), it narrowed this down further.
What we had to correct or verify: The first response was too generic since it didn’t account for the fact that we had already pushed. We had to give it our specific situation before the explanation became useful and we verified the actual fix ourselves by rendering and then pushing.



Usage 2: infer function syntax and programming support

Tool / model: Google Gemini
What it produced: A structured explanation of what each function in the infer practical template does (specify(), generate(), etc.). After we asked for a simpler explanation, it gave a clearer version. A draft of the infer code for the simulation test, based on our decision to switch from the theory-based t-test.
What we had to correct or verify: The first explanation was too complex to follow. After getting a simpler version and implementing it ourselves, we ran into errors from using the wrong variable names and using the functions wrong, which we then debugged with its help. We verified the final code produced the correct output by checking it against our actual research question. We also had to check the reps = 5000 and set.seed(42) were doing what we thought (shuffling 5000 times and keeping the shuffling the same each time the code is run) and made sure the filtering matched our RQ before trusting the output.



Usage 3: Grammar and phrasing in written sections

Tool / model: Claude
What it produced: Suggestions for fixing awkward sentence structure and word choice in written sections, while keeping our own meaning.
What we had to correct or verify: We explicitly asked it to keep our meaning and only fix grammar and word choice, since we sometimes used words that weren’t precise enough. We reviewed each suggestion and only kept changes that matched what we actually meant. Sometimes we changed word suggestions we felt unnatural with.

title: Munich Unemployment Analysis
text:
This project analyses unemployment trends across Munich’s 25 city districts from 2000 to 2024, using open data from the Munich Open Data Portal.
We explore two questions:

How has the unemployment percentage in Munich changed from 2000 to 2024 overall and by city area (North, East, South, West)?
Do unemployment percentages differ in 2024 based on gender across all 25 districts Munich.

Read the full analysis on the Report page, the initial Proposal or learn more About this project.

title: Munich Unemployment Analysis (2000 - 2024)
text: This statistical report explores the unemployment percentage across Munich’s 25 city districts over a 24-year period. By aggregating individual districts into broader geographic areas (North, East, South, West, and Center), we analyze regional trends over time. Additionally, we investigate, whether there is a difference in unemployment percentages between women and men in 2024 in Munich using inferential statistical methods.

title: Munich Unemployment Analysis (2000 - 2024)
text:
1. Introduction & Data Context

Source and Licensing
Our analysis uses data from the Munich Open Data Portal covering the years 2000 - 2024. The dataset is published under Datenlizenz Deutschland Namensnennung 2.0 and was downloaded from the Munich Open Data Portal on 2026-05-18. This open government license allows for copying, redistribution, and commercial usage as long as proper attribution is provided.


Dataset Variables
Each row in the dataset represents a specific subpopulation’s annual unemployment metric. In our analysis we focus on the variable Indikatorwert (unemployment percentage) along its Auspragung (demographic category) and Raumbezug (district).


Data Structure
Each row represents the yearly percentage of unemployed people per district in Munich, as well as the total yearly percentage across all districts, further divided into total, German, non-German, male, and female categories.
Columns include the id, indicator name, unemployed-person type (total, male, female, …), year, Munich district (including the city total), unemployment percentage, unemployed count, and total resident count (15–64 years old).
Row identification: _id, year (Jahr), district (Raumbezug), and demographic group (Auspragung) together uniquely identify each row.
Key variables we expect to use are the year, district, unemployment percentage, and both base values.


Initial Documentation Gaps
During our initial data screening, we identified documentation gaps in the raw dataset:
The Munich Open Data page gives a formula description but no real variable dictionary. Columns like Auspragung and Basiswert 1 / Basiswert 2 are left unexplained.
A separate metadata PDF from the Statistisches Amt München (https://stadt.muenchen.de/dam/jcr:42312f77-cae6-4995-a534-ff9dfff69480/indikatorenatlas_muenchen_metadaten_2023.pdf) reveals information not present in the file itself:
Basiswert 2 changes its meaning depending on Auspragung (whether the row concerns gender or nationality), and “Stadt München” rows aggregate geographic data differently than individual district rows.

Data quality issues
There are no major data quality issues. For our analysis we want to analyze Munich district areas separately. Therefore, we map Munich’s 25 districts (Raumbezug) into five areas based on our own judgment:

Center: Districts 1, 2, 3

East: Districts 5, 13, 14, 15, 16

library(tidyverse)
library(dplyr)

arbeitslosen_clean <- arbeitslosen |>
  filter(
    Auspragung == "insgesamt",
    str_detect(Raumbezug, "^[0-9]+")
  )

district_area <- tibble::tibble(
  district_code = c(
    1, 2, 3,                     # Center
    4, 11, 12, 24,                # North
    5, 13, 14, 15, 16,            # East
    6, 7, 17, 18, 19, 20,         # South
    8, 9, 10, 21, 22, 23, 25      # West

  area = c(
    rep("Center", 3),
    rep("North", 4),
    rep("East", 5),
    rep("South", 6),
    rep("West", 7)
  )
)

arbeitslosen_area <- arbeitslosen |>
  mutate(district_code = as.numeric(str_extract(Raumbezug, "^[0-9]+"))) |>
  left_join(district_area, by = "district_code") |>
  filter(!is.na(area), Auspragung == "insgesamt")


We chose to do an inline cleaning approach within this Quarto document to ensure reproducibility without outer dependencies. This way our project can be run by anyone, just by executing the .qmd file. Additionally, our programming is kept transparent and traceable.

title: Munich Unemployment Analysis (2000 - 2024)
section: 3. RQ1: Unemployment Trends by City Area (2000 - 2024)
text:
3. RQ1: Unemployment Trends by City Area (2000 - 2024)
Research Question 1: How has the unemployment percentage in Munich changed from 2000 to 2024 overall and when grouped by city area (Center, North, East, South, West)?

Interactive Trend Analysis
To analyze the unemployment trends over the 24 year period, we calculate the annual average of the unemployment percentage (Indikatorwert) across the mapped districts for each geographic city area. We also include the city-wide total (“Stadt München”).
The interactive line graph below displays these trends. You can hover over any data point to inspect the exact year and mean unemployment rate or double click the legend to isolate specific regions.


Show code
library(plotly)

annual_means_area <- arbeitslosen_area |>
  group_by(area, Jahr) |>
  summarise(mean_rate = mean(Indikatorwert, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

annual_means_total <- arbeitslosen |>
  filter(Auspragung == "insgesamt", Raumbezug == "Stadt München") |>
  transmute(area = "Total", Jahr, mean_rate = Indikatorwert)

annual_means_all <- bind_rows(annual_means_area, annual_means_total)

rq1_plot <- ggplot(annual_means_all, 
                   aes(x = Jahr, y = mean_rate, 
                       group = area, 
                       color = area,
                       text = paste0("Area: ", area, "<br>Year: ", Jahr, "<br>Rate: ", round(mean_rate, 2), "%"))) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(size = 1.2) +
  facet_wrap(~area, scales = "free_y") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Mean Unemployment Rate by City Area",
    x = "Year", y = "Mean Unemployment Rate (%)",
    color = "City Area",
    caption = "Source: Statistisches Amt München"
  ) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2")

ggplotly(rq1_plot, tooltip = "text")




Figure 1: Interactive mean unemployment rate by Munich city area, 2000-2024




Description and Interpretation of the Plot
All five regions and the city-wide total show a similar trend over the 24 year period. In 2000, almost all graphs start at around 3.6% before peaking dramatically in 2005 at almost 7% and only a small plateau in 2004. The center area graph has the exact same trend but remained consistently lower, peaking at approximately 6% in 2005. After 2005 all graphs show an inconsistent but almost identical decline to about 2.5% in 2019. In 2020, every area graph shows a drastic jump to about 4%, declines again in 2022 but increases from then on to 4% in 2024.
The peak in 2005 is explained by the introduction of the major Hartz IV welfare reforms in Germany that same year. The reform merged the statistical registries for traditional unemployment benefits and social welfare into a single metric, which artificially inflated the total count of registered unemployment individuals rather than reflecting a sudden economic collapse. After this, rules for receiving welfare became stricter and combined with a growing economy resulted in an inconsistent but steady decline in unemployment rates.
In 2020, this downward trend was abruptly interrupted by the COVID-19 pandemic, causing a sharp jump.

title: Munich Unemployment Analysis (2000 - 2024)
text:
4. RQ2: Gender Disparities in Munich (2024)
Research Question 2: Do unemployment percentages in 2024 differ significantly based on gender across Munich ?
To investigate whether there is a systematic difference between male and female unemployment rates in all districts of the city, we isolate the 2024 observation year.

Hypotheses
To test this statistically, we define our null and alternative hypotheses:
Null Hypothesis (\(H_0\)): In 2024, there is no difference in the mean unemployment rate between males and females in Munich. \[\mu_{\text{male}} = \mu_{\text{female}}\]
Alternative Hypothesis (\(H_A\)): In 2024, there is a significant difference in the mean unemployment rate between males and females in Munich.\[\mu_{\text{male}} \neq \mu_{\text{female}}\]


Descriptive Statistics
Before inferential testing, we generate a descriptive summary table using the {gt} package. This summarizes the sample sizes, averages, minimums and maximums of the unemployment rates for both demographic groups across all 25 districts.


Show code
library(gt)

arbeitslosen_2024_gender <- arbeitslosen |> 
  mutate(district_code = as.numeric(str_extract(Raumbezug, "^[0-9]+"))) |>
  left_join(district_area, by = "district_code") |>
  filter(
    Jahr == 2024,

Inferential Analysis (Permutation Test)
To compare the mean unemployment rates between males and females across of Munich, we analyze data from all \(n = 25\) districts per demographic group. While \(25\) observations per group provides a more robust dataset than a small sub region, it is still below the traditional threshold of \(n = 30\) of a normal distribution.
Because unemployment rates are naturally restricted by 0% on the lower end and tend to be skewed, relying on the parametric assumption of a classical two sample \(t\)-test remains risky. Therefore, we use a simulation based permutation test using the {infer} package.
This non parametric approach is completely distribution free. It constructs an empirical null distribution directly from our sample by shuffling the group labels (“männlich” and “weiblich”) 5000 times and calculating the difference in means. This computational method provides a \(p\)-value without making any unverifiable assumption about the population.


Show code
library(infer)
library(tidyverse)

unemployment_2024 <- arbeitslosen |>
  filter(
    Jahr == 2024,
    Auspragung %in% c("männlich", "weiblich")
  )

observed_diff <- unemployment_2024 |>
  specify(Indikatorwert ~ Auspragung) |>
  calculate(stat = "diff in means", order = c("männlich", "weiblich"))

set.seed(42)
null_dist <- unemployment_2024 |>
  specify(Indikatorwert ~ Auspragung) |>
  hypothesize(null = "independence") |>
  generate(reps = 5000, type = "permute") |>
  calculate(stat = "diff in means", order = c("männlich", "weiblich"))

p_value_val <- null_dist |>
  get_p_value(obs_stat = observed_diff, direction = "two-sided") |>
  pull(p_value)

null_dist |> 
  visualize() +
  shade_p_value(obs_stat = observed_diff, direction = "both", fill = "pink", color = "red") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Permutation Null Distribution (All Munich Districts)",
    subtitle = paste("Observed Difference =", round(observed_diff$stat, 3), "percentage points | p-value =", round(p_value_val, 3)),
    x = "Difference in Mean Unemployment Rate (Male - Female)",
    y = "Frequency"
  )





Figure 3: Simulated null distribution of the difference in means (5000 permutations) with the observeed statistic






Interpretation of the Permutation Test Results

Description: The red vertical represents the observed difference in mean unemployment rates (\(0.2\)). The pink shaded regions show the portion of the null distribution as extreme or more extreme than this result in both directions. The combined area of these pink regions equals the \(p\)-value of \(0.221\), which represents the probability of observing such a difference under the null hypothesis.
Observed Difference: In 2024, the observed difference in mean unemployment rates between men and women across all 25 districts of Munich is 0.2 percentage points (with males having a slightly higher average unemployment rate than females).
Null Distribution: Our generated null distribution represents a scenario where gender has no systematic impact on unemployment rates. Shuffling the gender 5000 times shows that differences around 0.2 percentage points are common under the null hypothesis.
The \(p\)-value: The two-sided permutation test produces a \(p\)-value of \(0.221\) or \(22.1\%\).
Statistical Decision: Because our \(p\)-value (\(0.221\)) is larger than the significance level \(\alpha = 0.05\), we fail to reject the null hypothesis (\(H_0\)).
Conclusion: We do not have statistically significant evidence of a gender disparity in unemployment rates across Munich’s districts in 2024. The observed difference of 0.2 percentage points is minor and can easily be explained by random district variation.

title: Munich Unemployment Analysis (2000 - 2024)
text:
5. Conclusion and Discussion
This analysis investigated both the trend of unemployment in Munich from 2000 to 2024 (RQ1) and potential gender based disparities in unemployment rates for the year 2024 (RQ2).

Key Findings

Trends (RQ1): The trend over the 24 year period revealed synchronized trends across all five Munich regions and the city wide total.
Gender Disparities (RQ2): Our initial focus on a subset of South Munich pointed toward a statistically significant gender gap. When increasing the analysis to all 25 districts, the difference was statistically insignificant and can easily be explained by natural / random variation.



Key Takeaways

Understanding context behind the data: The dramatic 2005 peak is a reminder that statistical anomalies are sometimes caused by administrative changes rather than real world crises. The Hartz IV reforms did not trigger a sudden economic collapse, they simply changed how we count the unemployed. This shows how important it is to understand the context of a dataset before rushing to conclusions.
Danger sample sizes: Comparing South Munich to the entire city shows how easily sample sizes can mislead. With only six districts in our initial southern subset, fluctuations were amplified. This made a gender gap look statistically significant by pure chance. When we looked at all of Munich, that gap disappeared.
Resampling: Unemployment rates are bounded ratios that are naturally skewed. That’s why relying on classical parametric tests like a \(t\)-test is risky with only 25 districts. By choosing a simulation based permutation test through the {infer} package, we didn’t have to worry about forcing our data into a normal distribution. Instead, we let the computer shuffle the data to give us a distribution free \(p\)-value we can actually trust.



Final Summary
In conclusion, although Munich’s unemployment trends have been shaped by policy shifts and global pandemics, there is no systemic gender gap in unemployment rates across the city.

title: Proposal
text:
The “Arbeitsmarkt - Arbeitslosen” dataset uses Datenlizenz Deutschland Namensnennung 2.0 (dl-by-de) (Version 2.0). This is a German open government licence: you can copy, use, and redistribute the data for any purpose, including commercially, as long as you attribute the source.



https://www.mstatistik-muenchen.de/indikatorenatlas/export/export.php



Rows: 3250
Columns: 10
Each row represents the yearly percentage of unemployed people per district in Munich, as well as the total yearly percentage across all districts, further divided into total, German, non-German, male, and female categories.
Columns include the id, indicator name, unemployed-person type (total, male, female, …), year, Munich district (including the city total), unemployment percentage, unemployed count, and total resident count (15–64 years old).
Row identification: year (Jahr), district (Raumbezug), and demographic group (Auspragung) together uniquely identify each row.
Key variables we expect to use are the year, district, unemployment percentage, and both base values.



Indikatorwert: the indicator value — not obvious from the name alone that this is the unemployment percentage, or which unit is missing (%).
Basiswert 1 / Basiswert 2: the names give no information about what the values mean.
Auspragung: the column name gives no information about the values it contains — a categorical variable for demographic/subpopulation groups.



The Munich Open Data page gives a formula description but no real variable dictionary. Columns like Auspragung and Basiswert 1 / Basiswert 2 are left unexplained.
A separate metadata PDF from the Statistisches Amt München (https://stadt.muenchen.de/dam/jcr:42312f77-cae6-4995-a534-ff9dfff69480/indikatorenatlas_muenchen_metadaten_2023.pdf) reveals information not present in the file itself: Basiswert 2 changes its meaning depending on Auspragung (whether the row concerns gender or nationality), and “Stadt München” rows aggregate geographic data differently than individual district rows.
A user who loads the dataset without tracking down this external PDF could misinterpret the metrics or miscalculate averages by mixing districts and city totals.




How has the unemployment percentage in Munich changed from 2000 to 2024 overall and by city area (North, East, South, West, Center)? → Descriptive
Do unemployment percentages differ in 2024 based on gender across districts in South Munich? → Explanatory

Cut:
We originally considered a third, predictive research question (“Can we build a simple model to predict the unemployment percentage in the next years?”). We cut this to keep scope focused on the two questions above. Also the updated proposal required to lock in at most two focused research questions.



Target audience: Agentur für Arbeit München, Jobcenter München, Referat für Arbeit und Wirtschaft der Landeshauptstadt München.
Purpose: analysis of trends in each district to plan resources and aid for unemployed citizens, and to design additional support such as seminars tailored to each district.
Presentation: statistical report



The dataset contains the unemployment percentage for each district and for the city of Munich overall. It lets us examine each district and the trend of the unemployment rate over 24 years. The advantage over raw unemployment counts is that the percentage immediately shows the relation to population size. The analysis matters because it shows the history of Munich’s unemployment and how different years and policy choices coincide with different outcomes for the city.

title: Proposal
section: RQ1: Unemployment trend by city area (2000–2024)
text:
RQ1: Unemployment trend by city area (2000–2024)
Specific claim: We estimate the trend of mean unemployment percentage over time (Indikatorwert ~ Jahr) grouped by city area and total, by calculating the annual average of the districts, and report line graphs showing the trend from 2000 to 2024.
Classify the analysis: Goal: Descriptive — we describe the state of Indikatorwert per city area from 2000–2024. Exploratory — after reviewing the available variables we fixed this question.
Model specification:

Response: Indikatorwert — unemployment percentage in % (<dbl>)
Predictor: Jahr — year (<dbl>), across six groups of Raumbezug (North, East, South, West, Center, Total)
Method: annual mean of the districts within each city area and city-wide total
Expected output: a summary dataset of annual means and line plots showing the trend from 2000 to 2024
Interpretation: this tells us how the unemployment rate has historically developed and shifted over a 24-year period across different regions of Munich

District → area mapping. Munich’s 25 districts don’t come pre-labeled with a compass region, so we assigned each district to North / East / South / West based on its approximate position relative to the city center. The two central districts (Altstadt-Lehel, Ludwigsvorstadt-Isarvorstadt) are excluded from the area breakdown since they sit at the center rather than in any one direction; they remain included in the city-wide total.

Note: this mapping is a judgment call we made, not an official designation.



Show code
district_area <- tibble::tibble(
  district_code = c(
    1, 2, 3,                     # Center
    4, 11, 12, 24,                # North
    5, 13, 14, 15, 16,            # East
    6, 7, 17, 18, 19, 20,         # South
    8, 9, 10, 21, 22, 23, 25      # West
  ),
  area = c(
    rep("Center", 3),
    rep("North", 4),
    rep("East", 5),
    rep("South", 6),
    rep("West", 7)
  )
)

arbeitslosen_area <- arbeitslosen %>%
  mutate(district_code = as.numeric(str_extract(Raumbezug, "^[0-9]+"))) %>%
  left_join(district_area, by = "district_code") %>%
  filter(!is.na(area), Auspragung == "insgesamt")

annual_means_area <- arbeitslosen_area %>%
  group_by(area, Jahr) %>%
  summarise(mean_rate = mean(Indikatorwert, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

annual_means_total <- arbeitslosen %>%
  filter(Auspragung == "insgesamt", Raumbezug == "Stadt München") %>%
  transmute(area = "Total", Jahr, mean_rate = Indikatorwert)

annual_means_all <- bind_rows(annual_means_area, annual_means_total)




Show code
ggplot(annual_means_all, aes(x = Jahr, y = mean_rate)) +
  geom_line(color = "steelblue", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(size = 1.2) +
  facet_wrap(~area, scales = "free_y") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Mean Unemployment Rate by City Area",
    x = "Year", y = "Mean Unemployment Rate (%)",
    caption = "Source: Statistisches Amt München"
  )

The interactive version lets readers hover over any point to read the exact year and mean unemployment rate for a given city area, rather than estimating values from the line alone.
Description: All five regions and the city total show a similar trend. In 2000 all graphs except the center area graph start at around 3.6% and drastically peak in 2005 with almost 7% and only a small decline in 2004. The center area graph has a similar trend but only peaks at around 6% in 2005. After 2005 all graphs show an inconsistent but almost identical decline to about 2.5% in 2019. In 2020 every area graph shows a drastic jump to about 4%, declines again in 2022 but increases from then on to 4% in 2024.
Interpretation: The peak in 2005 is explained by a newly introduced reform Hartz IV the same year that counted the unemployed and people on welfare into one number. After this, new rules were introduced where receiving welfare became stricter and so the numbers decreased again.

title: Proposal
section: RQ2: Gender differences in South Munich, 2024
text:
RQ2: Gender differences in South Munich, 2024
Specific claim: We compare mean unemployment percentage between männlich and weiblich groups (Indikatorwert ~ Auspragung) across South Munich districts for the year 2024, using a difference-in-means test, and report the estimated difference, a 95% confidence interval, and a p-value.
Classify the analysis: Goal: Explanatory — we ask whether Auspragung (gender) is related to Indikatorwert. Exploratory — after seeing the data was already grouped by gender, we decided to investigate further.
Model specification:

Response: Indikatorwert — unemployment percentage in % (<dbl>)
Predictor: Auspragung — categorical grouping filtered to männlich & weiblich (<chr>)
Method: difference in means between the two groups, using a theory-based t null distribution
Expected output: the observed difference in means, a null-distribution plot with the observed statistic shaded, a p-value, and a 95% CI
Interpretation: this tells us whether male residents face systematically higher or lower unemployment percentages compared to female residents across South Munich districts in 2024

Show code
library(infer)

south_districts <- c(6, 7, 17, 18, 19, 20)

arbeitslosen_south <- arbeitslosen |>
  mutate(district_code = as.numeric(str_extract(Raumbezug, "^[0-9]+"))) |>
  filter(district_code %in% south_districts)

obs_stat <- arbeitslosen_south |>
  filter(Jahr == 2024 & Auspragung %in% c("männlich", "weiblich")) |>
  specify(Indikatorwert ~ Auspragung) |>
  calculate(
    stat = "diff in means",
    order = c("männlich", "weiblich")
  )

null_dist <- arbeitslosen_south |>
  filter(Auspragung %in% c("männlich", "weiblich")) |>
  specify(Indikatorwert ~ Auspragung) |>
  assume("t")

null_dist |>
  visualize() +
  shade_p_value(obs_stat = obs_stat, direction = "both")









Result interpretation: the difference in average unemployment rate between männlich and weiblich in 2024 falls within the high-probability region of the null distribution. The variation between groups can be explained by random sampling variation, and we cannot reject the null hypothesis that there is no systematic gender gap.
